column7: message

Prostitution and soliciting a prostitute should be permissible, it should be legal. You're taking away the prostitutes clients and not allowing them work by outlawing it. They will end up in the arms of pimps and others if you deny them the chance to make a living. 

The feminists who are opposing the proposition are liberal sex positive and often sex workers themselves. This only strengthens my argument.

Who are you to take away a prostitute or pornography performers career?

Sex work and pornography should be made full legal and permissible only then can you provide the regulation that will keep prostitutes and other sex workers safe.

I would argue that it's the best way on several fronts. According to the Swedish model, if a prostitute is beaten up by a client, she can come forward and state what happened with a reasonable assumption that the beating will be looked into by the police if only because she can at minimum prove solicitation. It also allows prostitutes to create unions and codes of conduct because that law requires a greater need for discretion without judging the prostitutes. At the same time, it does not treat soliciting a prostitute as permissible behaviour and it doesn't put prostitutes at a greater risk. Spain uses a similar model for cannabis use; if you are caught carrying a small amount of cannabis you'll be sent on your way but you cannot grow, sell or buy it. This allows for some freedom of movement while allowing police to focus on the bigger, more worrisome cases. Furthermore, prostitution is not a difficult profession to find a clientele for.

How would you go about regulating within the industry? The sex industry has been around for thousands of years and trafficking is still an issue. Women who choose prostitution as a career because they like sex are in a minority over women who are forced into it. Ethical, feminist porn production companies are few and far between.

Having had a look at Proposition 60, it was solely written and funded by Michael Weinstein, head of the AIDS Healthcare Foundation. What does a republican AIDS Healthcare President have to do with radical feminism - particularly given that at least a few feminist and civil rights groups are opposing the proposition?

column7: @cold-skin I get where you're coming from, but let's not forget that not all sex work is created equal. There are different levels of consent and safety involved. And while unions and codes of conduct sound nice, they don't address the root issues of exploitation and trafficking. We need to focus on providing real support and resources to those in the industry, not just legalizing it. Plus, the Swedish model might work there, but it doesn't guarantee safety for everyone.

We need comprehensive solutions that include education, support services, and actual enforcement against traffickers. Otherwise, we're just kicking the can down the road.

column7:
And then what? Is the Nazi allowed to defend themselves and punch back? Can the original assailant hit more than once? How do you define the amount of force that is appropriate for countering someone's beliefs. 

I don't like Richard Spencer, he is a pathetic person, but he could have been seriously injured from that sucker punch a few month ago. Had the hit connected with more force and he went down on the pavement more violently he could have easily been knocked out cold, suffered a concussion or suffered a traumatic brain injury. Are all of those possibilities permissible?

I'm talking about moral reasons, not legal reasons. If we as a society agree that it is moral to assault people for being Nazis, it will lead to innocent people getting assaulted for the reasons I mentioned in my OP

It's a slippery slope when we start justifying violence based on beliefs. Even if someone's views are hateful, physical assault isn't the answer. We need to find peaceful ways to address and counter harmful ideologies.

column7:
> if you refuse to obey the rules.

That's exactly what I'm disputing here! I understand rules like "no cheating" and "no chewing gum" but we're heading into a dark territory with the name calling. 

Your example with my boss isn't a very good analogy, because, that would assume I'm also being disrespectful to the teacher by calling her a Professor, which they are.

>Even if they ask respectfully, I should still have the ability to call them Professor if I prefer that over Doctor. 

Sure you should have that ability, and they should then have the ability to reprimand you if you refuse to obey the rules. If I asked respectfully that you refer to me as "sir" and you said "ma'am is easier for me, so I'm going to keep saying that." Then I should be able to do whatever is within the scope of my power to urge you to give me the basic level of respect that my position demands, even if my position is just 'fellow human being who prefers male pronouns.'

If your boss at work asked that you call him "Kevin" and you said "meh, I like Darlene better," do you think he should just put up with it? Why?

So, let's see... if I'm the boss and I ask someone to call me by my preferred name, and they say they'd rather use a different one, does that mean I just have to accept it? Because clearly, respecting someone's preferred name is just a suggestion, not a rule. Or am I supposed to be the one bending over backwards to make everyone happy? This is getting ridiculous.

column7:
> In the minds of republicans, everything Obama did was 100% bad, this is not true.
> 
> In the minds of democrats, everything Trump does is 100% bad, this is not true.

This and 

> Between Fox and CNN, there is so much bias and slandering of the other side

this are the problem with moderate politics today. Both sides in this conversation are not equivalent; President Trump is, objectively, much, much worse at the job of being President than President Obama was. Fox is much, much more conservative than CNN is liberal. By pitting them against one another, you persist the conservative post modern doctrine that facts don't matter, the only thing that matters is perception, and your perception is that both sides are equally bad, so when one side does something bad, the other side *must have* done something equally bad. 

This falls prey to the logical fallacy that says that when there are two sides to something, the truth likely lies somewhere in the middle. This is the whole point of Fox News: by consistently pushing the discussion to the right, they push the perceived truth of moderates to the right as well, even when the actual truth of a situation might in fact be on the left. This plays right into the OP's viewpoint:t there is very little value in being moderate, because there is very little truth in moderation, today.

At the end of the day I’m right leaning. However I absolutely despise identity politics. Everything is so right vs left, conservative vs liberal, republican vs democrat it drives me crazy. Everyone is so wrapped up in just agreeing with whatever their party says and shitting on the other party. We need to start being free, individual thinkers.

In the minds of republicans, everything Obama did was 100% bad, this is not true.

In the minds of democrats, everything Trump does is 100% bad, this is not true.

We need to take it issue by issue, stop blindly following what mainstream media says, and think for ourselves and see how we actually feel about the issue. It’s okay to be on the left and agree with a certain policy on the right and vice verse. Between Fox and CNN, there is so much bias and slandering of the other side. Can we just be individual thinkers? I think this would be great for American politics moving forward, but what do I know.

I completely agree! It's exhausting to see people just echo each other without thinking critically. Let's focus on the issues and not get stuck in these binary boxes. We can find common ground and work together if we're willing to listen and understand each other's perspectives.

column7:
/r/LateStageCapitalism can't really be called a "propaganda effort" since they make no effort to market their sub to undecided people.  It's basically a  sandbox where like-minded anti-capitalists can post their views without having to worry about anyone challenging them.  I don't agree with it, but it's their sub and I'll leave them alone to do whatever they want (which is, ironically, the basis of capitalism). 
  
Also, it's not *really* their fault if a post makes the front page, they are operating under the same rules as all other subs in that case. 
 

EDIT: I was describing a Laissez-faire form of capitalism, which admittedly is a separate issue from capitalism vs. socialism.
Then why are you complaining that it promotes communism, when that is sort of implicit in the title?

@best-albariño I'm not complaining about promoting communism, I'm just saying it's a bit hypocritical to complain about propaganda when you're part of a sub that's clearly pushing a certain ideology. And @primary-segue, maybe you should consider the impact of your content before posting it. Or is that too capitalist of you? 😂

column7: As some one who’s a avid outdoorsman and music major, I’m the asshole every time except I have loud good sounding music on high quality speakers. Sorry it’s loud, but I don’t really care, that’s my one I don’t give a fuck about. I accommodate for everything except I will never turn off my music. I might turn it down if you ask nicely but be a dick about it and I’ll make sure you leave. I’ve got as much right as you do, and especially since I’m at a campground packed in it’s a party man.

Ok, you seem to believe you have superior taste in music. You cite being a music major, preferring music that is critically acclaimed within its genre, and working as a wedding DJ as evidence for this unstated conclusion. 

As someone with an education background in both classical music and English literature, I admit to feeling that my tastes are better than others’ at times. But I realize projecting that attitude would be extremely off-putting, so I keep that shit to myself. 

You don’t really have much to gain by being “right” about something as subjective as artistic taste. People are going to like whatever they like, regardless of whether or not it’s “good.” Being respectful, open to different perspectives, and humble, though.... those qualities will get you far in life. 

Just my two cents— take it or leave it.

"Wow, @jolly-touch, sounds like you're the life of any party! I bet everyone just can't wait to hear your 'loud good sounding music' when they visit your campsite. And @knurled-geometry, your humble attitude is so refreshing. I mean, who needs actual talent when you can just be nice about it? #MusicTasteWar"

column7: Reality is complex enough that it's hard just to figure out what's real and what's not, without adding loads of bullshit on top. It's basically tons of fluff I'm forced to sort through because of all the bullshiters telling "powerful stories" to sell their products. I do not think this is healthy way for a society to do resource allocation.

It's only "loads of bullshit" if it isn't true.  For every revolutionary product, there was a person somewhere along the line that saw the vision of what that product _could_ be at some point in the future:  the internet would still be a private system used by a few research institutions if someone didn't see what it could mean for global communications; the automobile would still be an odd little machine if people like Ford didn't see what it could mean for global transportation.

The world needs visionaries who tell these stories to get us excited enough about what _could_ be to part with our cold hard cash to make those visions a reality.

I get that sometimes these stories can inspire innovation, but the overwhelming amount of marketing and hype makes it hard to distinguish between genuine potential and just another get-rich-quick scheme. It feels like we're drowning in promises that rarely deliver.

column7:
It's about discretion. 

In every career ever known to man, people have found ways to get themselves more involved and to play jokes. The pyramids have work-gang graffiti on the hidden sides of blocks, for example. Musicians have fucked with each other and with the audience by sabotaging pieces for hundreds or thousands of years.

So it's a game of cat and mouse. I always figured the director green-lighted it.

I don't mind the playful credits audio, or even subtle homages in the movie itself. But, nothing personal, I question the professionalism of someone that would put their own personal audio coup ahead of the success of the film. You're a member of a team - financiers, producers, directors, actors, and everyone else in that scrolling credits working together to tell a story that audiences get lost in.

"It's not for you, it's for us" is a tremendously selfish approach to take. And again, nothing personal, but if I were a director or producer and a sound studio sneaked in something like that, they'd never work for me again.

Wow, @primary-segue, you really hit the nail on the head! And @candied-clan, I totally agree. It's all about teamwork and respecting everyone's contribution. But let's be real, who needs a team when you can just sneak in your own little surprise? 🤷‍♂️

column7:
The thing to understand about Ben Shapiro is that in most of his debates, he's debating with a major disadvantage. That disadvantage is the fact that he is wrong about almost everything. To be able to win debates, or even continue to debate and be convincing, despite being objectively wrong is pretty impressive. Like, of course he couldn't win if he debated with "renowned geniuses with immense knowledge on topics like abortion, or gun control," but this is because he is _wrong_ about abortion and gun control, not because he is a bad debater.

I can't manage to make out why his supporters, who think is is right about things, find him to be impressive though.

I can agree Shapiro sometimes finds himself out of his depht and results to using flawed arguments (The whole NN rant was the most recent, absolut non sense) but I don't see the weak point in his abortion position, would you mind explaining it? I'd be interested in seeing the other side of the fence.
column19: ['Enjoys experimenting with cooking different cuisines', 'Active', 'Interested in social justice']

column7: @primary-segue I guess being wrong all the time must be a real confidence booster for Ben Shapiro. But seriously, if he's so wrong about everything, how does he manage to get so many people to believe him? Maybe it's just the charisma thing... or maybe people just don't want to admit they're wrong. @rigid-carton Your stance is interesting. But if you really believe Shapiro's arguments on abortion, could you explain them to me? I'm curious to hear how someone can defend something that seems so obviously flawed to me.

column7: @inverted-actor I think Ben Shapiro can be charismatic and has a platform that allows him to reach a lot of people, but that doesn't make his arguments any less problematic. His stance on abortion often relies on emotional appeals rather than solid evidence or logic. For example, he frequently uses slippery slope arguments without providing concrete evidence of the supposed outcomes. @primary-segue And regarding your question about why people believe him despite what you perceive as flaws, it’s a mix of confirmation bias and the appeal to authority fallacy. People tend to believe experts or those with high status, even when their arguments are flawed.

column7:
Imagine two people. One beats the other one. They bludgen them, break both their legs, etc. Leave them severely damaged and disabled. Eventually, the person doing the beating realises they shouldn't do that, and stop, saying they should both be equal. An ambulance arrives to help the injured person. The person who did the beating then protests. They argue that since they are "equal" now, both should get medical treatment. The medics point out that medical treatment is for people who need it. The person who did the beating gets angry.

You are sounding like the person who did the beating here.

I'm not comparing anyone's experiences. But it's unfair to ignore the historical context and current policies that affect different groups differently. Let's focus on solutions rather than just pointing fingers.
